primer.markdown:
# You have scaffolded, prompted, streamed, looped, and cached. Now wrap it and ship.

The five modules before this one handed you every part of an agent: a package and key (01), a persona and a tool schema (02), a token-by-token renderer (03), a `while (true)` loop keyed on `stop_reason` (04), and a `cache_control` annotation that makes the loop affordable (05). There is nothing left to teach about *how to build* an agent. What is left is to wrap the thing in a CLI shell and publish it.

The cookbooks/RESEARCH-AGENT.md example on lines 222-262 shows the archetype: a small class (`ResearchOrchestrator`) that composes three agent helpers (`PlanningAgent`, `ResearchAgent`, `SynthesisAgent`), exposes one public method (`conductResearch(topic)`), and runs from a top-level `async function main()` that reads from `process.env.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` and prints the result. That is a shippable CLI. Rename the class, swap the helpers for your own, add a `#!/usr/bin/env node` shebang plus a `bin` entry in `package.json`, and you have a published command.

This module is the canonical last module of every LUXOR Academy Quest. You paste a URL to your own repo — your research agent, your code reviewer, your email triager, your weather bot — and the Academy's codebase-to-course pipeline extracts the same six slots from *your* code: a primer anchored in your README, a visual traced from your architecture, an interactive sandbox pointed at your real CLI, an artifact deep-link into your canonical file, a self-check derived from your tests, and a next-step that routes a future learner through *your* codebase.

What you get back is a permalink. Not a certificate. Not a badge. A URL that teaches someone else the agent you just built, with your name on the attribution line. Phase 1 of LUXOR Academy captures your repo URL and queues it for the pipeline. XP vests in two steps — **150 XP lands the moment you queue your repo URL** here in Phase 1; a Phase 3 bonus unlocks when the pipeline mints the permalink for your generated Quest.
primer.key_insight: The last module of every Quest is not a summary. It is a handoff: you paste your repo URL, the pipeline runs the same six-slot contract on it, and you walk away with a permalink that teaches the thing you just built.

visual.alt: A pipeline flow diagram. At the left, a repo URL icon (github.com/you/your-agent) enters a three-gear mechanism labelled `extract → validate → render`. Between the gears and the output, the six slot labels hover as tags: primer, visual, interactive, artifact, self_check, next. At the right, the pipeline emits a permalink ribbon stamped with the learner's handle, and a module card showing the six slots populated from the learner's own code.
visual.alt_es: Un diagrama de flujo de tuberia. A la izquierda, un icono de URL de repo (github.com/tu/tu-agente) entra en un mecanismo de tres engranajes etiquetado `extract → validate → render`. Entre los engranajes y la salida, las seis etiquetas de slot flotan como tags: primer, visual, interactive, artifact, self_check, next. A la derecha, la tuberia emite una cinta de permalink estampada con el handle del estudiante, y una tarjeta de modulo mostrando los seis slots poblados desde el codigo propio del estudiante.

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline' https://fonts.googleapis.com; script-src 'unsafe-inline'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner pastes a GitHub repo URL into the form, reviews the preview of what the pipeline would extract (the six slots derived from their repo), and submits. Phase 1 stores the URL locally for Phase 3 pipeline activation; the submit button is a visual stub in Phase 1.

questions.prompt: In cookbooks/RESEARCH-AGENT.md lines 222-262, the `ResearchOrchestrator` composes three helper agents in its constructor. What are they, in order?
questions.explanation: RESEARCH-AGENT.md lines 228-230: `this.planner = new PlanningAgent(apiKey); this.researcher = new ResearchAgent(apiKey); this.synthesizer = new SynthesisAgent(apiKey);`. The three-stage orchestration — plan, research in parallel, synthesize — is the shape of most shippable agents. Rename the stages and you have your own CLI.

questions.prompt: Why is the canonical last module of every LUXOR Academy Quest a 'Ship Your Own' step rather than a summary quiz?
options: Because summary quizzes are harder to grade automatically.
Because learners should end every Quest holding a permalink to their own work, not a score — the Quest trains for the *next* repo, not for itself.
Because the XP award is larger for shipping than for testing.
questions.explanation: The sovereignty clause. Per the Academy meta-prompt and MERCURIO's canonical-last-module pattern, each Quest ends by running the same six-slot contract on the learner's own repo and emitting a permalink back. Summary quizzes train for the Quest. Ship-Your-Own trains for the next repo.

questions.prompt: `ResearchOrchestrator.conductResearch(topic)` on RESEARCH-AGENT.md lines 234-259 runs the three research helpers with one specific concurrency shape. Which?
options: All three stages run sequentially, one after the other.
All three stages run in parallel with `Promise.all`.
Plan runs first, then the research calls for every subtopic run in parallel via `Promise.all`, then synthesis runs last on the gathered findings.
questions.explanation: RESEARCH-AGENT.md lines 238-246: `subtopics = await this.planner.planResearch(topic)` runs first (sequential), then `Promise.all(subtopics.map(...))` fans out the research calls in parallel, and finally `this.synthesizer.synthesize(topic, findings)` runs sequentially on the joined results. This plan-fanout-join shape generalises to almost any multi-stage agent CLI you will ship.
